candidates.stmt: As a long-term local resident, I'm committed to ensuring our community remains a great place to live and work for all. With over 20 years of business experience and an MBE for services to social enterprise, I'll use my skills and knowledge to fight for improved transport, services and local decision-making.

Having grown up in Somerset and Devon and lived in Them since, Alphington is now my home. I have run and been part of campaigns in Cornwall, Devon and Somerset to deliver genuinely affordable, Sustainable homes. I have been a board member of Plymouth Energy community Homes and would like to facilitate appropriate community values to protect our green spaces, enhance them and keep them clean. As well campaign to facilitate appropriate infrastructure for walking, cycling and bus which will all serve Alphington. Living in the local area means I care about it and the people I meet. I won’t just see you on the doorstep; I will see you in and around the Village.

Kayleigh lives and works west of the river, so your concerns are her concerns.
She is a strong local campaigner, in 2019 persuading BT to take down unsightly broadband poles in Exwick.
Kayleigh will fight to ensure you get better public services; toilets re-opened, solutions to dog fouling, parks and playgrounds better maintained. She says: “As an independently minded Conservative, I pledge to listen to residents and get the changes which Exwick needs”. 
She also wants to see unused council land ‘rewilded’, bus shelters installed to protect public transport users and will push for the introduction of long-promised kerbside glass recycling.

I will ensure that the council works to maintain the balanced community that we have in Alphington, through careful scrutiny of proposed developments, championing community projects and enhancing local services.

I am campaigning to be your city councillor for Alphington. I want the village to be the *crown jewel* of Exeter – the place that everyone else envies – with the best services, housing and small businesses in the city.

The Liberal Democrats have a strong record across Exeter and Devon, including in neighbouring Teignbridge and more recently Devon County Council. I wish to continue this trend and be a *community champion* for the village and surrounding area, bringing *transparency and strong governance values* to the table to strengthen the link between the council and residents.

I put community at the heart of everything I do, and I want residents to feel a part of a strong one. I work as a tennis coach across the city, providing free community tennis sessions to people of all ages and backgrounds. In my work, I champion inclusion and access to our green spaces and would continue to do so as your councillor.

I will ensure that the council works to maintain the balanced community that we have in Alphington, through careful scrutiny of proposed developments, championing community projects and enhancing local services. I would be a councillor that will stand up and advocate for the area and ensure that the voices of residents are heard at the Civic Centre. I hope I can count on your support on 7th May.

Kevin is a reliable community champion who works hard and keeps in touch all year round. He was first elected to Exeter City Council in 2003 and also works as a nurse at the Royal Devon and Exeter Hospital. He has a track record of dedicated public service.

Kevin and his ward colleague, Cllr Michael Mitchell, always put our area first. They get things done for Duryard and St James.

Voters in Duryard and St James deserve better than being taken for granted by Labour. They deserve better than yet another Labour councillor, who will just go along with what their party bosses tell them to do.

Kevin holds the Labour-run City Council to account to get the best deal for local people. He will always put our area first, not his party whip.

Elections here are a close race between the Lib Dems and Labour. Many people who have voted Conservative in the past are backing Kevin. A vote for the Tories here will only let Labour win.

Please use your vote for Lib Dem Kevin Mitchell on Thursday 5th May to get our area the strong voice on the council that it deserves.

Having served 9 years in the RAF as an Electronics Engineer, I have a keen eye for detail and a strong commitment to serving others. A resident of Exwick for over 14 years, I am a single parent currently home educating my teenage daughter to spare her the damaging effects of our broken education system.
Our community here in Exwick is often overlooked, whether that's parking issues, weeds, or missed bin collections, and I share your frustration with these problems. But with a strong voice for Exwick on the City Council, who knows our part of the city well and has its best interests at heart, there is good reason to be optimistic about Exwick's future.

Exeter City Council is not working for Exwick. Their 'Exeter Plan' deliberately sets out to minimise space for cars. For example, they have allocated the area in front of St Davids station, including its car parks, for a 'low-car' residential development, and don't indicate how much parking for the station will be retained. That risks more station users treating Exwick like a park and ride.

They increased parking charges in the city centre by an average of 7.4% last year - while borrowing over £1 million to install solar panels on buildings.
They do not take your needs and concerns in to consideration.

I live and work locally in Exeter and I'm fed up seeing Labours damaging incompetence nationally and locally make a mess of things. Locally Labour councillors have voted to close public toilets and allow playgrounds to deteriorate, all whilst having one of the worst recycling rates in the South West and also saddling Exeter City residents with over £IOO million of debt. Then, after hiking parking charges, they are closing roads all over the city causing disruption and pollution. Conservative run councils run better services with less cost. Birmingham is a classic an example.

If elected I will be campaigning for the basic stuff people want, public toilets to be re-opened; the budget for parks, playgrounds and green spaces to be increased, more bins for dog-poo and litter, to introduce glass recycling. Cycling routes should be increased, but not by closing off roads, Low Traffic Neighbourhoods LTN’s should be only allowed if the MAJORITY of residence want it, and not imposed by councillors that don’t feel the adverse affects.

Lets do what the Exeter and Devon public want, not what the ideological left think they want!

Stella has lived and worked in Exeter for around 15 years and works as a heritage consultant. Throughout her career she has developed a strong commitment to protecting and improving our shared historic environment, and Exeter provides an exceptional setting in which to undertake this work. Stella says: “I firmly believe that everyone should be treated with fairness and respect, and that all members of our community should have equal access to opportunities and essential services. The Green Party reflects my own values and aspirations for a fairer, more inclusive society.

I have lived in Exeter for 15 years and work in project delivery at the University. I moved to Heavitree a few years ago and am a regular visitor to the Pleasure Grounds with my son.  I am very lucky to work with a diverse group of people, finding effective, evidence-based solutions to things that aren’t working well, and I would like to use my skills and experience to improve our local services and access to green spaces.  Our community in Heavitree is precious, and there is more that unites us than divides us.  Let's keep it that way!

Proud Professional with A Career in the NHS & Lifelong Resident of Whipton, Exeter

I have lived in Whipton my entire life, building a career through working in our great NHS, meeting and talking with people from all walks of life, caring for people who are needy and vulnerable.  I know how public services such as the NHS work, how they should work as well as the impact when they do and don’t.  

I am concerned that the local services provided have not been given the attention that they are due.  As your candidate for Reform UK, I am committed to using my experience to hear all your voices to understand not only your concerns but also to know and celebrate our triumphs and achievements.

I promise to listen to everyone’s opinion, to discuss with openness and respect, to move forward together for the future, so that our voices are heard to make the necessary influence to deliver the changes necessary to improve our local community.

I was born in and still live in Heavitree, where my parents have lived for over 60 years. I worked as a civil servant for 28 years and realised then how much of our money is wasted on abandoned projects and new initiatives that never take off, like the failed Exeter City Living Ltd scheme. I also strongly oppose the Low Traffic Neighbourhood scheme that was inflicted upon Heavitree. How can forcing more traffic to already busy roads be eco-friendly? I am concerned that a vote for Labour or the Greens will open the door for bringing back LTN's.

I've spent my career helping businesses - big and small - and I'm actively involved in local organisations, including as a trustee for Age UK Exeter. I will bring my skills and experience to ensure Devon County Council delivers value for money, invests in our neighbourhoods, supports local businesses, and protects our environment.

I am a retired NHS Healthcare Scientist, working at the RD&E Hospital for over 20 years. With lifelong experience in community leadership roles, including as a school governor, I am committed to improving the quality of life for people in Exeter. 
It has been deeply frustrating for residents that there has, in reality, been no-one representing Heavitree and Whipton on Devon County Council. Labour Councillor 
Danny Barnes having missed 11 out of the 16 meetings he should have attended from 24th January 2024 to date. 
Too much money has been wasted on road schemes in Exeter without proper public consultation. It was great that local campaigning got the Low Traffic Neighbourhood removed. However, we are still left with the cycle lane on Rifford Road that has created floating bus-stops which are a known hazard to the blind and partially sighted.” The Labour Highways Committee not only supported these schemes, but were spineless and did nothing to shout out for local residents. I will speak up—I will consult and I will put local residents at the heart of everything that I do.”

I decided to get involved in local government because I believe our community deserves stronger representation, practical solutions, and a voice that truly listens to residents.

Like many people in our area, I’ve seen the growing challenges we face — from the cost of living and local services to concerns about safety, housing, and accountability. I didn’t want to sit back and complain; I wanted to step forward and be part of making things better.

As an ongoing Reform UK councillor, I have been committed to standing up for local residents, challenging wasteful spending, and pushing for greater transparency and accountability. I will continue to work tirelessly to ensure that local voices are heard and that local decisions reflect the needs and priorities of the community, not distant agendas.

I have had strong ties to St Sidwells and St James for around 50 years, with my children all attending Newtown first school, two of whom are dyslexic and one autistic, and now all 3 are hard working, tax paying, family men.

I left home at 16, married at 17, was widowed at 22, had lengthy periods of unemployment, returned to education in my late 20s, became a self employed taxi driver and started a property business.  I now manage the properties and also work on a zero hours contract for a Royal Mail Group company.  

I have worked hard to make ends meet and to become financially sustainable, and have even been a trade union member for most of my working life.  I can see, however that although the mainstream parties have now abandoned my families’ values and any form of common sense, I am thrilled that the Reform UK Party has not!

My work in providing housing for local people has enabled me to be passionate about local issues such as parking, taxation, social care and special needs education!  I want to offer the electorate an alternative vision of common-sense local governance that directly benefits the entire community and supports local values rather than out-of-touch ideologies which only cater to a few.

"Gill has been active in the Exeter community for over 18 years. In 2016, 2018 and 2019 she stood as our City Council candidate in Heavitree, receiving the second highest Green vote of anywhere in the city. In addition to this, Gill has carried out vital work on the impact of air pollution, and setup Friends of Mincinglake and Exeter Greenspace Group.  Gill is a manager at the  RD&E hospital.  She is deeply caring and a true servant of her community.

Having lived in exeter for many years and Pennsylvania for 22 years, I see both the challenges facing exeter and the opportunities to improve people’s lives here. Things are tough for many of us right now especially with the ongoing cost of living crisis.I am now for the first time hoping to win your vote in order to be a strong voice for Pennsylvania on the city council. It would be my greatest honor to represent Pennsylvania.

I thrive on giving back to my community. As well as working as a teacher, I have served as a charity trustee and Chair of Governors to a primary school. My priorities include housing needs, open spaces, community infrastructure, and tackling anti-social behaviour in our neighbourhoods.

I first became a Councillor in 1995. Initially as a Dartmouth Town Councillor & South Hams District Councillor. Including as the Deputy Mayor of Dartmouth, Chairman Corporate Property Committee, Chairman Finance and General-Purpose Committee, a member the Planning Committee on both councils,  and also served on the Housing Committee.

During this period my duties included as a non-Executive Director at Tor Housing Association, Dartmouth Primary School governor ,Trustee Dartmouth Special Needs Group , Dartmouth Community College School governor , St Johns Roman Catholic School governor, founder member Dartmouth Youth Council , Dartmouth Youth Club Management Board , Dartmouth Swimming Pool Management Committee, trustee Dartmouth United Charities ,trustee Dartmouth Trust Housing Association ,and Chairman Dartmouth St Johns Ambulance.

I have been a member of Exeter City Councillor since 2003 and Devon County Council since 2005.

In terms of Exeter City Council, my previous experience includes as the Lord Mayor of Exeter, Deputy Lord Mayor, and Lord Mayors Consort. Portfolio Holder for Environment and Leisure, Portfolio Holder for Housing and Community Involvement, Portfolio Holder Housing Revenue Account, Portfolio Holder for Place, and Chair of the Council Governance Review Panel. Currently a member of the Planning Committee, two scrutiny committees and Chair of the Council Housing & Advisory Board.

In terms of Devon County Council, previously Chairman School Transport Appeals Committee, member County Wide Adoption Panel, delegate to the Children’s Safeguarding Improvement Board and Vice Chairman Peoples Scrutiny, Chair Adult Services Overview Group, member of the Children’s Services Overview Group, Devon Waste and Recycling Committee, and Development Management Committee. Also previous chair and long-standing member of Exeter Highways and Traffic Orders Committee.

Currently Leader of the Labour Group, Chair of Children’s Scrutiny, Chair Children’s Servic

I have lived here for 26 years and for the past decade it has been a real privilege to serve Pinhoe as one of your City Councillors.

I am standing again at this election because I care deeply about our community and I believe Pinhoe deserves councillors who live here, understand first-hand the issues we face, and will keep standing up for local residents.

As your Councillor, I have got a proud track record. I have helped secure investment in projects such as Pinhoe Hub, the Station Road enhancement, its accessible play park, the BMX pump track and skatepark. I am also directly involved with local groups, charities and residents’ associations.

More recently I also helped establish the Save Devon’s Libraries campaign, challenging Lib Dem and Green-run County Council cuts that threaten valued local services like Pinhoe Library.

If you re-elect me, I promise that I’ll keep working hard for you:

I’m proud to live in Pinhoe and Exeter, and while both can be improved, I will keep working to make Pinhoe the best it can be. My record speaks for itself.

I’ll fight to ensure the council delivers value for money, keeping council tax low, with just 8% of your council tax going to the City Council to fund our services and facilities.

I’ll work with the Council, police and local groups to support community projects and initiatives, while tackling fly-tipping, litter and antisocial behaviour in Pinhoe.

I hope I can earn your support at this upcoming election.

I believe local government has a huge, if often unnoticed, impact on people's everyday lives. Having studied economics, I am particularly passionate about the fiscal aspects of local government, and would seek to stop the council running a deficit, to put an end to their fiscal mismanagement and, to make taxes as fair as possible for all working people.
So many of us are completely dissafected with the establishment parties and with good reason.
Pinhoe deserves better. It deserves reform

I am proud to have been born and raised in Exeter, where my family has deep roots. Having lived here all my life, I care deeply about our community’s future. My experience travelling, running a business, and working as a ski teacher and national coach has helped me connect with people from all walks of life. I am standing as a councillor to ensure local voices are heard. I will be approachable, accountable, and focused on practical solutions. I am not a career politician—I am one of you, committed to making a difference.

I’m Jack Vickers, your local Green candidate for Priory. I’m proud to be standing for election on 7 May.

It’s been great meeting so many of you, and I’m very grateful for the support people continue to show me here. Speaking with residents on the doorstep has helped me better understand the issues people face day to day. Here is some important information ahead of the election in a few weeks.

I lived on Burnthouse Lane for years, and if there’s one thing I remember about living in the area, it’s the widespread feeling that we’d been neglected and forgotten by the council. Too often our neighbourhood hasn’t had the attention or investment it deserves, and many residents tell me they feel the same today.
I have worked many jobs over the years, from warehousing to retail to security, and I know how to work in a team to deliver results.

I am passionate about tackling the lack of affordable housing in our city, as well as improving transport and cycling infrastructure. I am also committed to protecting our green spaces, such as Ludwell Valley Park, so they can be enjoyed by local families for generations to come.

Philip Thomas moved to Exeter five years ago and has lived in Priory Ward for four of those years. He works as a physicist at the University of Exeter and volunteers at his local church.

Philip is a strong supporter of transparent, collaborative decision making in local government. If elected councillor, one of his priorities will be to work hard to improve the Council's transparency and accountability. He has spoken to other Priory Ward residents who feel taken for granted by the Labour Council, for example regarding the handling of the development of Edwards Court.

My day job is as a professor at the University of Exeter, where I teach criminology. My work has led to collaborations with local and other police services across the UK and internationally. I want to use this experiences to work with the existing team of Green councillors in the ward and focus on community safety. I am also a committed trade unionist. I have served as branch President and Secretary of the University and College Union among many other posts. I believe every resident deserves dignity and respect — and I will always work to make sure your voice is heard.

To find out about the priorities of the Green Party in St David's and Exeter more widely, visit: https://exeter.greenparty.org.uk/why-green/.

I’m Rose Lelliott and I am delighted to be standing again as your Labour and Co-operative candidate in St David’s.

I work as a policy researcher and live locally in the ward. I love calling this beautiful part of Exeter home.

I have a track record of standing up for our community, such as supporting the gold Britain in Bloom award, speaking out against the Mallison Bridge cut and getting potholes filled.

I am currently leading the Keep Exeter Lit Campaign against Devon County Council’s streetlight dimming policy, which has received local and national media coverage, alongside becoming the biggest ever petition on Devon County Council’s website.

As a councillor, I will continue to speak up for residents, using  my experience of campaigning to be a hardworking and approachable local representative.

I was born in Exeter and have been a resident of Heavitree for the last 30 years.

I have worked for Royal Mail for 38 years, so have first hand knowledge of how our roads and pavements have deteriorated over the years.

We all know that traffic volumes are a problem. A more integrated and reliable public transport system is needed in Devon to reduce the need for people to use their cars.

The safety of pedestrians, schoolchildren and cyclists also requires addressing. We need a choice of proposals for which everyone who may be affected can have their opinions heard before a suitable choice is selected.

Born and raised in Exeter, and with most of my immediate family still living close by, I have always considered this city home. It should be a place we’re proud of and where people want to visit, but it has sadly become a hollowed-out city centre: endless vape and charity shops, litter, drugs, anti-social behaviour… We can’t continue like this.

The good news is we have the opportunity to change things for the better.

After qualifying as a solicitor and pursuing a career in international finance, including over a decade in FinTech helping expanding micro-businesses all over the world to succeed, I now want to use my experience to transform our city for the better. My background in law and business means I’m well qualified to scrutinise budgets, contracts, and outsourcing, and ensure taxpayers’ money is spent as effectively as possible.

I still love Exeter — I know it really well and am a keen local historian — and I would love to see it restored to a prosperous, thriving, well-maintained regional business hub, and a place worth visiting.

Vanessa has lived in Exeter for 22 years and has a grown-up son who lives in London. She was an Exeter City Councillor for 6 years, and a Devon County Councillor for 7 years until 2013. During this time she championed children and young people, was very involved with health and adult social services, and was instrumental in getting the new Haven Banks Outdoor Education Centre built on the Quay. She also represented her residents in the smaller things that matter, like getting someone a disabled parking bay, getting respite care for the husband of an elderly lady who was at the end of her tether, and strongly opposing a proposed Park and Ride site in her patch. As a governor of a Children’s Centre, and of a Federation of two schools in Exeterx she is concerned about education and the poor funding per pupil received by Devon schools. Vanessa strongly supported the Remain campaign, but is concerned to listen to, and represent all the residents of Exeter.

As a born and bred Devonian, I have an undying love for our county and for our city—its beauty, history, and resilience. No matter what is thrown its way, it always recovers. But it deserves more than that; it deserves to thrive.

Witnessing the decline of my local area—shops shutting, streets decaying, and the rise of anti-social behavior, which has been left unchecked by authorities—has persuaded me to no longer be an observer, and instead to take
an active role in improving where we live and making it everything we all wish it could be.

I’ve had the privilege of serving as one of your St Thomas councillors for the past eight years, and I’m proud of the improvements we’ve achieved together by listening to your ideas and acting on your priorities.
We’re now approaching a significant period of change. Over the next two years, the City Council will be preparing to become part of a new unitary authority. Elections for that new council will take place in May 2027, followed by a ‘shadow authority’ that will operate until the City Council is fully incorporated in 2028.
During a transition of this scale, having a strong, experienced voice representing St Thomas will be essential. I hope you’ll place your trust in me to be that voice. Please judge me on the track record I’ve built as your Labour councillor—and on my continued ambition for St Thomas as we move into this next chapter.

Ann is a research scientist, and has lived and worked in Exeter since 2004.

One of her key interests is transport and encouraging better coordination between the City and County councils to make it easier for everyone to travel in more sustainable ways. She is also concerned about development on the city’s vital green spaces.
